response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean validation accuracy and standard deviation across five random seeds for each configuration. The results are summarized as follows: variant_01 (67.2 ± 0.8), variant_02 (66.9 ± 0.7), variant_03 (67.8 ± 0.6), variant_04 (65.1 ± 0.9), variant_05 (66.3 ± 1.0), variant_06 (64.7 ± 0.7), variant_07 (65.8 ± 0.8), variant_08 (70.4 ± 0.5), variant_09 (66.5 ± 0.7), and variant_10 (65.9 ± 0.8). Variant_03 serves as our baseline, corresponding to the original model without the proposed component, and achieves a validation accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6.

Among all tested configurations, only variant_08 surpasses the baseline, achieving a validation accuracy of 70.4 ± 0.5. This represents an absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline, and is the highest performance observed among all variants. In contrast, the remaining proposed variants (variant_01, variant_02, variant_04, variant_05, variant_06, variant_07, variant_09, and variant_10) do not outperform the baseline, with accuracies ranging from 64.7 to 67.2. Most notably, variant_06 exhibits the lowest validation accuracy at 64.7 ± 0.7, substantially underperforming relative to the baseline.

These results indicate that, while the majority of the proposed variants fail to improve upon the baseline, variant_08 stands out as a promising configuration, demonstrating both higher accuracy and reduced variance across seeds. This suggests that the specific combination of modifications in variant_08 is critical for realizing the potential benefits of the proposed method. Further analysis of variant_08 may yield insights into which aspects contribute most to the observed performance gains.
